questions.prompt: A project has EV=$80,000 and AC=$95,000. What is the Cost Variance (CV)?
A project has SPI = 0.75. This means:
choices: The project is progressing at 75% of the planned rate — behind schedule

questions.prompt: Which formula gives EAC assuming current cost efficiency continues?
VAC (Variance at Completion) is calculated as:
Planned Value (PV) is best defined as:
choices: The actual amount spent to date
The budget value of work actually completed
The authorised budget assigned to work scheduled to be done by a specific date

questions.prompt: A project with CPI=1.15 and SPI=0.90 is BEST described as:
choices: Over budget and ahead of schedule
Under budget and ahead of schedule
How much work remains divided by time remaining
The cost efficiency needed to complete remaining work within the original budget
The schedule variance forecast at project end
How much budget has been consumed to date

questions.prompt: Which EVM metric answers 'How much will the project cost at completion if current trends continue?'

choices: The total project budget approved at start
The difference between planned and actual costs
The remaining estimated cost to finish the project
The variance between budget and actual at project end

questions.prompt: SV = EV - PV. If SV is POSITIVE, the project is:
Which is NOT a valid interpretation of CPI = 0.85?
choices: For every $1 spent, $0.85 of value is delivered
The project will likely finish under budget
The project is experiencing a cost overrun trend

questions.prompt: What does it mean when both CV and SV are negative?
choices: Under budget and ahead of schedule
Over budget AND behind schedule simultaneously
The project will finish early with savings

questions.prompt: A project reports: CPI = 0.78, SPI = 0.85, EAC = $640,000, BAC = $500,000. What is the PM's MOST appropriate corrective action?
choices: Accept the overrun — EAC is only an estimate, wait until project end
Immediately rebaseline the project to match EAC so variances disappear
Escalate to sponsor, investigate root causes of cost inefficiency, explore scope reduction or resource reallocation to recover
Add more resources to speed up the schedule, which will automatically fix the cost overrun
questions.explanation: CPI = 0.78 means $1 of work costs $1.28 — serious cost inefficiency. SPI = 0.85 means schedule is also behind. The corrective action sequence is: (1) escalate to sponsor (overrun is significant), (2) investigate ROOT CAUSE — is it scope creep, inefficient resources, rework, procurement issues?, (3) consider crashing high-priority tasks if schedule slippage is causing cascading costs, (4) explore cutting low-priority scope. Rebasing hides the problem. Adding resources often worsens cost efficiency.

questions.prompt: A project has BAC = $300,000 and EAC = $375,000. VAC = -$75,000. The project is 60% complete. Which statement BEST describes the PM's responsibility?
choices: VAC is negative, meaning the project is $75,000 under budget — no action needed
VAC = -$75,000 means the project is forecasted to overrun by $75,000; the PM must report this to the sponsor and actively seek corrective actions
The PM should wait until project completion to determine if VAC is accurate
A negative VAC simply means the EAC formula was applied incorrectly
questions.explanation: VAC = BAC - EAC = $300,000 - $375,000 = -$75,000. Negative VAC means the project is forecasted to OVERRUN by $75,000 — it will cost $75,000 more than the approved budget. The PM's responsibility is to: (1) report the forecasted overrun to the project sponsor immediately, (2) identify what is driving the cost overrun, (3) propose corrective actions (reduce scope, improve efficiency, request budget increase). Doing nothing or waiting is NOT acceptable project management practice.
